identity.title: Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance

lens.included_activities: Independent repair and maintenance of consumer electronics, computers, office machines, communications equipment, and scientific, medical, and other electronic or precision instruments without primarily retailing new equipment.
lens.excluded_activities: New-equipment retailers, captive manufacturer service, appliance and automotive repair, software-only support, manufacturing, and remanufacturing that changes intended use or performance.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer depot work, business service calls, maintenance contracts, warranty subcontracting, field service, calibration, and regulated equipment service generate incident, hourly, fixed-price, and contract revenue.

a.bridge: The source population is the broader electronic repair workforce plus adjacent electronics inspection. Occupational composition and AI-assisted inspection are translated into addressable diagnosis, triage, documentation, scheduling, retrieval, and inspection labor. Manufacturing inspection has greater repeatability and biases capability upward; workforce data hide digital diagnosis within repair roles and can bias downward.
a.rationale: Diagnosis and information processing are important, while office, engineering, sales, and production work add digital tasks. Disassembly, soldering, calibration, testing, and field handling remain physical.
a.change_evidence: A task-level study across medical, communications, computer, office-machine, and consumer-electronics repair would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Manufacturing inspection is adjacent rather than direct repair evidence.

rho.bridge: The source population is AI-assisted electronics inspection and regulated medical-device service. Demonstrated inspection capability and controlled service-information needs are mapped to feasible diagnosis and workflow realization. Standardized production images bias upward relative to heterogeneous repair, while proprietary tools and regulated validation reduce deployment.
rho.rationale: AI can assist inspection, pattern recognition, triage, and retrieval. Fragmented data, proprietary diagnostics, sparse failure labels, cybersecurity, liability, and quality systems constrain use.
rho.change_evidence: Independent field data on AI diagnostic use, first-time-fix improvement, technician acceptance, and validation by equipment segment would materially change the value.
caveats: Medical controls are not representative of consumer repair.
Feasibility does not establish safe autonomy.

e.rationale: Field service, depot repair, calibration, and maintenance can be externally provided. OEM warranties, integrated service organizations, in-house technicians, replacement, and proprietary access reduce contestability.
e.change_evidence: Segmented spending data identifying captive, in-house, authorized third-party, and independent provision would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The frozen firm count is absent, so downstream opportunity counts cannot be grounded.
Externalization differs sharply by equipment type.

s5.bridge: The source population is U.S. small-business owners across industries reporting near-term exits. The general propensity is mapped to this class and widened for unknown size, OEM authorization, customer concentration, and technical-key-person dependence. Nonemployers and unrelated sectors create unknown-direction bias; authorization complexity likely reduces actionable supply.
s5.rationale: Retirement creates sellers, but consumer shops may be subscale and precision firms often depend on specialists, credentials, and customer approvals.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age, succession, closure, and transaction data for 811210 by equipment type would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Exit intent is not a marketable business.
No direct six-digit transaction series was located.

q.rationale: Fixed-price repair, service contracts, improved first-time fix, faster triage, and technician leverage enable capture. Competitive bidding, OEM rates, warranties, procurement, and replacement economics pass value through.
q.change_evidence: Matched job data linking AI to price, resolution time, repeat visits, utilization, and gross margin by segment would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Capture may appear as uptime or compliance.

d5.bridge: The source populations are relevant BLS electronic repair occupations and medical-equipment repairers. Their mixed employment outlooks are translated into five-year service demand. Employment can diverge through productivity and outsourcing; fast medical growth biases a simple aggregate upward while declining office and communications repair biases it downward for other mixes.
d5.rationale: Medical repair is favorable while several computer, office, communications, and commercial-electronics occupations are flat or declining. Complexity and uptime support demand; cheap replacement and OEM control offset it.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 811210 receipts and work orders by device category with installed-base and replacement data would materially change the value.
caveats: Employment is an imperfect demand proxy.
Segment mix can dominate the aggregate.

o.rationale: Precision repair generally requires equipment custody, physical access, test instruments, parts, calibration, and accountable records. Remote diagnosis, modular swaps, software fixes, and replacement can remove some operator work.
o.change_evidence: Segment evidence on remote resolution, automated calibration, modular swaps, abandonment, and autonomous repair would materially change the value.
caveats: Persistence is lower for disposable consumer devices.
It is higher for medical and installed scientific equipment.

narrative.executive_view: A valuable precision-service core is combined with challenged consumer and office repair; heterogeneity, proprietary access, and the absent firm count weaken the aggregate thesis.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve triage, visual inspection, document retrieval, fault recognition, dispatch, and reporting, while physical repair and validation remain constrained.
narrative.value_capture: First-time-fix and technician leverage support value, but OEM schedules, procurement, warranties, and replacement cost constrain capture.
narrative.firm_availability: Transferability depends on credentials, authorization, customers, and specialists; the absent frozen firm count prevents a grounded quantity estimate.
narrative.demand_durability: Medical service is favorable while several office and communications repair occupations contract, producing modest aggregate growth with wide uncertainty.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Consumer electronics, computers, communications, medical devices, and scientific instruments have materially different regulation, product life, and economics.
narrative.principal_driver: Growing need to maintain and document complex installed precision equipment, especially medical equipment.
narrative.principal_weakness: Extreme segment heterogeneity, proprietary restrictions, and the declared firm-count gap.

sources.supports: Supports AI inspection capability and implementation barriers.

sources.title: Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
